392.text: If a pass is thrown or attempted before the thrower acknowledges the call or the call is made after the throwing attempt, and

393.label: 17.C.6.b.1.
393.text: if possession reverts to the thrower, all players return to the locations they occupied at the earlier of:

396.label: 17.C.6.b.2.
396.text: if a player other than the thrower is awarded possession due to an uncontested call, all players return to the locations they occupied at the time of the infraction.

397.label: 17.C.6.b.3.
397.text: if the result of a play stands, players return to the locations they occupied when play stopped. In addition, an infracted player is then allowed to move to recover the relative position lost because of the infraction.

398.label: 17.D.
398.text: When a call is made, throwers must stop play by visibly or audibly communicating the stoppage as soon as they are aware of the call.

399.label: 17.E.
399.text: If it is unclear whether a catch was made before the disc contacted the ground, or whether a player’s first point of ground contact after catching the disc was in- or out-of-bounds or in or out of the end zone, the player with the best perspective makes the call.

400.label: 17.F.
400.text: If a player makes a play-halting call and subsequently determines that their call was incorrect, they should retract the call by announcing “retracted.” Play still stops. Play restarts with a check and the stall resumes according to 15.F.1.

401.label: 17.G.
401.text: If a dispute arises concerning an infraction or the outcome of a play (e.g., a catch where no one had a good perspective), and the teams cannot come to a satisfactory resolution, play stops, and the disc is returned to the thrower and put into play with a check (10.D.3), with the count reached plus one or at six if over five.

402.label: 17.H.
402.text: If an infraction results in possession reverting to a thrower who was airborne when releasing the disc, play restarts at the spot on the playing field closest to the point of release.

403.label: 17.I.
403.text: If multiple calls occur on the same play or before play stops, the outcomes should be resolved in reverse sequence (latest call first, earliest call last).

404.label: 17.I.1.
404.text: If an earlier infraction is determined not to have affected the play (17.C.1 and 17.C.5), or is retracted (17.F), that infraction is not resolved and it does not affect the resolution of subsequent infractions.

405.label: 17.I.2.
405.text: Offsetting infractions: If both offensive and defensive infractions occur simultaneously or the sequence cannot be determined, the resolution for this set of infractions is: the disc is returned to the thrower and put into play with a check, with the count reached plus one or at six if over five.

406.label: 17.I.3.
406.text: Exception: If separate fouls occur while the disc is in the air, but before any player starts to attempt to catch or block the disc, those fouls are treated as simultaneous and on the same play.

407.label: 17.J.
407.text: A player’s ability to catch or make a play on the disc is not considered to be “affected” because that player stopped, slowed down, or otherwise ceased to continue playing because a call was made by another player. Players are encouraged to make every effort to continue playing until play actually stops.

408.label: 18.A.
408.text: After catching a pass, a player is required to come to a stop as quickly as possible and establish a pivot.

409.label: 18.B.
409.text: If a player catches the disc while running or jumping, the player may release a pass without attempting to stop and without setting a pivot, provided the player does not speed up or change direction while in possession of the disc and releases a pass before three additional points of ground contact occur, provided that all points of ground contact are completely in-bounds, excluding their attacking end zone (18.E.2).

410.label: 18.C.
410.text: A player may bobble the disc in order to gain control of it.

412.label: 18.D.1.
412.text: The thrower fails to establish and continually maintain a pivot at the appropriate spot on the field until the throw is released.

413.label: 18.D.2.
413.text: The thrower fails to touch the disc to the ground when required.

414.label: 18.D.3.
414.text: An offensive player advances in any direction an in-bounds disc that is rolling, sliding, or sitting on the ground.

415.label: 18.D.3.a.
415.text: If the defense commits this infraction, the thrower may inform the defense of the infraction without stopping play. The thrower must indicate where the infraction occurred and put the disc into play at that spot.

416.label: 18.D.4.
416.text: A player catches the disc and either does not stop as quickly as possible, speeds up, or changes direction before establishing a pivot.

417.label: 18.D.5.
417.text: A player purposefully bobbles the disc (including tipping, delaying, guiding, brushing, or the like) to oneself in order to advance the disc in any direction from where it initially was contacted.

418.label: 18.D.5.a.
418.text: If a defender commits this infraction on a disc in flight and the result of the play is the defender or the defender’s team gaining possession, the defender gains or retains possession at the initial spot of the infraction and becomes the thrower.

420.label: 18.E.1.
420.text: If a non-standing player loses contact with the pivot spot in order to stand up, it is not a travel, provided the new pivot is established at the same location.

421.label: 18.E.2.
421.text: It is not a travel if a player catches the disc while running or jumping, does not speed up or change direction while in possession of the disc, and releases a pass before three additional points of ground contact occur, provided that all points of ground contact are completely in-bounds, excluding their attacking end zone. In this instance, a player is not required to establish a pivot or stop as quickly as possible.

422.label: 18.E.3.
422.text: If play stops, the thrower may reset the pivot.

424.label: 18.F.1.
424.text: If the travel occurs and a pass is thrown:

425.label: 18.F.1.a.
425.text: If the pass is incomplete, play continues uninterrupted (as per the Continuation Rule, 17.C.4.b).

426.label: 18.F.1.b.
426.text: If the pass is complete, play stops and the disc is returned to the thrower where the travel occurred (as per the Continuation Rule, 17.C.4.a.1). Play restarts with a check.

427.label: 18.F.1.b.1.
427.text: If the thrower threw from either the end zone or out-of-bounds, and their pivot was more than one step from the appropriate spot, the following procedure is used instead: First, the disc is checked in where the thrower threw the pass, making it live, then the thrower walks to the appropriate spot for their pivot and puts the disc in play.

428.label: 18.F.2.
428.text: If the travel occurs and no pass has been attempted:

429.label: 18.F.2.a.
429.text: Play does not stop. The defense (typically the marker) points to the spot where the travel occurred, and the thrower returns to that spot without delay. The thrower must touch the disc to the ground before attempting a legal pass.

430.label: 18.F.2.b.
430.text: The stall count is paused until the thrower sets a pivot where the travel occurred. The marker is not required to say “stalling” when resuming the count if they previously initiated the stall count by saying “stalling” prior to calling travel. The thrower must touch the disc to the ground before attempting a pass.

431.label: 18.F.2.c.
431.text: If the thrower wishes to contest the travel call, they announce “violation” and play stops. Upon checking the disc in, the stall count resumes at the count reached plus 1, or 6 if over 5 (15.F.1.c).

432.label: 19.A.
432.text: A pick occurs whenever an offensive player moves in a manner that causes a defensive player guarding (3.D) an offensive player to be obstructed by another player. Obstruction may result from contact with, or the need to avoid, the obstructing player. However, it is not a pick if both the offensive player being guarded and the obstructing player are making a play on the disc at the time of the obstruction.

433.label: 19.B.
433.text: A pick can be called only by the obstructed player and must be announced by loudly calling “pick” immediately after it occurs.

434.label: 19.C.
434.text: If play stops according to 17.C, players reposition according to 17.C.6. In addition, the obstructed player is then allowed to move to recover the relative position lost because of the pick.

435.label: 19.D.
435.text: During any stoppage, opposing players may agree to slightly adjust their locations to avoid potential imminent picks.

436.label: 20.A.
436.text: It is the responsibility of all players to avoid initiating contact in every way possible.

437.label: 20.B.
437.text: The rules encourage and anticipate highly competitive play. This type of play inherently involves potential danger and risk of injury, even when players act responsibly within the rules. The standard rules provide resolutions for such events. The Dangerous Play Rule addresses behavior that lies outside this inherent level of danger and risk — behavior that cannot be accepted as part of the game. As such, the rule addresses the behavior itself, not the effect of the behavior on the outcome of a play, unlike the standard rules. The Dangerous Play Rule is not intended to address every situation involving potential danger or risk of injury, nor is it designed to guarantee safety. Dangerous Play. Actions demonstrating reckless disregard for the safety of or posing a significant risk of injury to fellow players, or other dangerously aggressive behavior are considered “dangerous play” and are treated as a foul. The proper call in such circumstances is “dangerous play” and play stops. This rule is not superseded by any other rule.

438.label: 20.B.1.
438.text: Dangerous play is considered a foul regardless of whether or when the disc arrives or contact occurs.

439.label: 20.B.1.a.
439.text: In general, a dangerous play call may only be made if at least some amount of contact between the involved players occurred, and the vast majority of dangerous play will involve contact between players. However, when the other criteria of Rule 20.B are met, contact is not required for a player to invoke this rule where there is near certainty that significant contact would have occurred had the player not taken last moment steps to avoid contact.

440.label: 20.B.2.
440.text: Resolution. If uncontested, a call of “dangerous play” is resolved as an analogous foul (e.g., if the call occurred while or immediately after the calling player was making a play on a disc in the air, it is treated as a Receiving Foul (20.E.2)). A player called for dangerous play may contest the call if they believe the call was incorrect (17.B).

441.label: 20.B.2.a.
441.text: Dangerous play between a thrower and marker is treated as a throwing foul that affected the play, regardless of whether or when the disc is released or when contact occurs, unless the calling player determines otherwise.

442.label: 20.B.2.b.
442.text: Dangerous play occurring when or immediately after the disc is in the air is treated as a receiving foul if either player involved is attempting a play on the disc. However, the calling player may elect to treat the dangerous play as a general foul, if the player determines that the dangerous play was unrelated to the overall play that decided the outcome of the action.

443.label: 20.B.2.c.
443.text: Dangerous play is treated as a general foul only if it occurs when the disc is not in the air, occurs far away from the disc, when the disc is obviously uncatchable, or when the calling player has elected such treatment under 20.B.2.b. In this situation, the calling player determines whether the play was affected, under the standard enunciated in this rule and its annotations.

444.label: 20.C.
444.text: A foul can be called only by the fouled player and must be announced by loudly calling “foul” immediately after it occurs.

445.label: 20.D.
445.text: Non-incidental contact resulting from adjacent opposing players vying for the same unoccupied position may be treated as offsetting fouls.

446.label: 20.E.
446.text: Some fouls carry some extra provisions, as listed below.

448.label: 20.E.1.a.
448.text: A throwing foul may be called when there is non-incidental contact between the thrower and marker. The disc in a thrower’s possession is considered part of the thrower.

449.label: 20.E.1.b.
449.text: In general, any contact between the thrower and the extended (i.e., away from the midline of the body) arms or legs of a marker is a foul on the marker, unless the contacted area of the marker is completely stationary and in a legal position.

450.label: 20.E.1.c.
450.text: Any contact that occurs due to the marker setting up in an illegal position (16.H) is a foul on the marker. This contact must be part of an ultimate-related maneuver (throwing, pivoting, etc.) and must occur with a part of the marker that is illegally positioned. Once the marker has set up in a legal marking position, it is the responsibility of both players to respect this legal position. However, contact resulting from the thrower and the marker both vying for the same unoccupied position is a foul on the marker.

451.label: 20.E.1.d.
451.text: Any contact initiated by a thrower with the body (excluding arms and legs extended from the midline of the body) of a legally positioned (16.H) marker is a foul on the thrower.

452.label: 20.E.1.e.
452.text: Although it should be avoided whenever possible, incidental contact occurring during the follow-through (after the disc is released) is not a foul.

453.label: 20.E.1.f.
453.text: If non-incidental contact occurs between the thrower and marker such that the thrower could call a throwing foul on the marker, the thrower may instead choose to call “contact.” Play does not stop and the marker resumes the stall count at “one.” Other than resetting the stall count, the “contact” call is resolved in the same manner as a marking violation (16). The marker may contest the “contact” call by calling “violation,” which is treated as the call of an offensive violation. If the thrower calls “contact” after beginning the throwing motion and subsequently releases the disc, it is treated as if the thrower called “foul.”

454.label: 20.E.1.g.
454.text: Any references above to a marker also apply to any defensive player within ten feet of the thrower’s pivot.

455.label: 20.E.1.h.
455.text: Before a thrower has established a pivot, application of throwing foul protections are limited to contact during the throwing motion and related windups. Instead, the general foul rules and responsibility to avoid contact will apply, and the player initiating non-incidental contact will generally have committed the foul, with the exception of blocking fouls (20.E.3).

457.label: 20.E.2.a.
457.text: If a player contacts an opponent while the disc is in the air and thereby interferes with that opponent’s attempt to make a play on the disc, that player has committed a receiving foul. Some amount of incidental contact before, during, or immediately after the attempt often is unavoidable and is not a foul.

458.label: 20.E.2.b.
458.text: If 17.C.3.b.1 or 17.C.4.a.1 of the Continuation Rule applies: if the call is uncontested, the fouled player gains possession at the spot on the playing field closest to the spot of the infraction. If the foul is contested, the disc reverts to the thrower.

459.label: 20.E.2.c.
459.text: The Principle of Verticality: All players have the right to enter the air space immediately above their torso to make a play on a thrown disc. If non-incidental contact occurs in the airspace immediately above a player before the outcome of the play is determined (e.g., before possession is gained or an incomplete pass is effected), it is a foul on the player entering the vertical space of the other player.

460.label: 20.E.2.d.
460.text: Force-out Foul: If an airborne player catches the disc and is contacted by an opposing player before landing, and that contact causes the player to land out-of-bounds instead of in-bounds, or out of the end zone instead of in the end zone, it is a foul on the opposing player and the fouled player retains possession at the spot of the foul. If an uncontested force-out foul results in an in-bounds player landing outside the end zone being attacked when they would have landed in the end zone without the foul, a goal is awarded.

462.label: 20.E.3.a.
462.text: When the disc is in the air a player may not move in a manner solely to prevent an opponent from taking an unoccupied path to the disc and any resulting non-incidental contact is a foul on the blocking player which is treated like a receiving foul (20.E.2).

463.label: 20.E.3.b.
463.text: A player may not take a position that is unavoidable by a moving opponent when time, distance, and line of sight are considered. Non-incidental contact resulting from taking such a position is a foul on the blocking player.

464.label: 20.E.4.
464.text: Strip: If a foul causes a player to lose possession of the disc, it is a strip. A strip is a subset of fouls and is treated the same way.

465.label: 21.A.
465.text: Each player is entitled to occupy any position on the field not occupied by an opposing player, unless specifically overridden elsewhere, provided that no personal contact is caused in taking such a position.

466.label: 21.B.
466.text: A player who jumps is entitled to land at the take-off spot without hindrance by opponents. That player also is entitled to land at another spot, provided that the landing spot, and the direct path between the take-off and landing spots, were not already occupied at the time of take-off.

467.label: 21.C.
467.text: The defense must not move in a manner that obstructs the offense from taking possession of the disc or establishing a pivot.

468.label: 21.D.
468.text: Players may not use their extended arms or legs to obstruct the movement of an opponent.

469.label: 22.A.
469.text: Observers may be used if desired by the captains or the event organizer.

470.label: 22.B.
470.text: Observers may perform any or all of the following duties:

471.label: 22.B.1.
471.text: Track time limits and announce associated warnings and expirations.

473.label: 22.B.2.a.
473.text: Any player directly involved in a dispute may request observer resolution.

474.label: 22.B.2.b.
474.text: An observer may resolve a dispute without request from the players involved if they cannot resolve it in a timely manner.

475.label: 22.B.2.c.
475.text: If an observer is involved in resolving a dispute, play restarts with a check.

476.label: 22.B.3.
476.text: Censure or eject players for Spirit of the Game infractions. This includes assigning responsibility for game delays to a specific player.

477.label: 22.B.4.
477.text: Render opinions on other on-field events (e.g., line and offside calls), as determined in advance by the event organizer.

478.label: 22.C.
478.text: By playing under observers, the players agree to abide by the observers’ decisions.

479.label: 22.D.
479.text: Players may overrule an observer’s active ruling, provided it is to their team’s detriment. Players may also decline enforcement of a yardage penalty assessed through the misconduct system. Observers may deny the declination if they feel the teams are trying to circumvent mandatory tournament rules, guidelines, or player safety.

480.label: 23.A.
480.text: If an infraction is committed and not called, the player committing the infraction should inform the infracted player or team of the infraction.

481.label: 23.B.
481.text: On a stoppage of play, if it is ever unclear which of a team’s members are the current players or where they are on or off the field, they should identify themselves when the opposing team requests.

482.label: 23.C.
482.text: If a dispute arises on the field, play stops and is restarted with a check when the matter is resolved.

483.label: 23.D.
483.text: If a novice player commits an infraction out of sincere ignorance of the rules, it should be common practice to stop play and explain the infraction.

484.label: 23.E.
484.text: In addition to the assumption that players will not intentionally violate the rules, players are similarly expected to make every effort to avoid violating them.

486.label: B1.A.
486.text: The default personnel ratio for the mixed division is 4/3 (four woman-matching and three man-matching or four man-matching and three woman-matching).

487.label: B1.B.
487.text: Prescribed Ratio Rule (ABBA): At the start of the game, after the first disc flip, an additional disc flip happens with the winner selecting the gender ratio for the first point. For the second and third points the ratio must be the reverse of the first point. For the fourth and fifth points the ratio must be the same as the first point. This pattern of alternating the ratio every two points repeats until the end of the game. Halftime does not impact the pattern. This is the preferred Personnel Ratio Rule for sanctioned play.

488.label: B1.C.
488.text: Endzone Decides Ratio Rule: At the start of the game, after the first disc flip, an additional disc flip happens with the winner selecting which end zone is “end zone A,” with the other end zone becoming “end zone B.” At the start of each point in the first half, the team that is defending end zone A must choose the mixed personnel ratio. For the second half, at the start of each point the team that is defending end zone B must choose the mixed personnel ratio.

489.label: B1.D.
489.text: Receiving Team Decides Ratio Rule: At the start of each point in the game, the receiving team must choose the mixed personnel ratio.

490.label: B1.E.
490.text: If the Endzone Decides Ratio Rule or the Receiving Team Decides Ratio Rule is in effect for the game, the team responsible for signaling the personnel ratio for the upcoming point must do so within twenty five (25) seconds after the previous goal was scored.

491.label: B1.F.
491.text: If a team cannot match the on-field personnel ratio of all seven players for the point, then it must play with fewer than seven players, but no less than five, matching the personnel ratio with as many players as possible.

492.label: B1.G.
492.text: Personnel Ratio Violation: If the disc has been pulled and a team has the incorrect personnel ratio, it is a violation.

493.label: B1.G.1.
493.text: Violations of the mixed personnel ratio and how they are handled after the pull are described in 2026-2027 Official Rules of Ultimate (9.C.1).

495.label: B2.A.
495.text: If Gender Match Signaling is to be requested, team and/or spirit captains must discuss and agree to do so during the captains’ meeting prior to the start of the game. If teams do not reach agreement, then players will signal gender as described in B2.B. Exception: If it becomes apparent, after the start of the game, that Gender Match Signalling is needed, team and/or spirit captains will need to meet and mutually agree for both sides to begin doing so. No player, captain, or coach may make a spur-of-the-moment request for the opposing team to signal their respective gender matches between points.

496.label: B2.B.
496.text: Players on the receiving team will signal their gender match (either “man-matching” or “woman-matching”) within 50 seconds after the previous goal was scored for the purpose of allowing the pulling team to match up defensively. The pulling team is permitted to remind the receiving team to signal their respective gender matches as long as it was previously agreed upon, per B2.A.

499.label: B3.A.1.
499.text: Each point, a player representing the gender match-up with four players on the field must pull for that point. That is, if there are four women-matching players then a woman-matching player must pull. This is the preferred rule for sanctioned play.

500.label: B3.A.2.
500.text: Majority Pull Violation: Violations of the gender match pull rule will be handled the same as an offsides violation (9.D.4), but instead of calling “offsides,” a player should call “majority pull violation.” Like with offsides violations, after the first majority pull violation, any subsequent majority pull violations should be handled as described in 9.D.4. Majority Pull Rule violations accumulate together with offsides and false start violations as “pull violations.” In games with observers, either the observer or a player on the field may call the violation.

501.label: B3.B.
501.text: If the Majority Pull Rule is not used, then any of the players on the pulling team may pull.

503.label: B4.A.
503.text: Substituting players must be of the same gender match as the player they are replacing.

505.label: C1.A.
505.text: Any observer may assess a Technical Foul, blue card, yellow card, or red card.

506.label: C1.A.1.
506.text: The infraction must have been witnessed by at least one observer.

507.label: C1.A.2.
507.text: The infraction must have occurred during the game.

508.label: C1.A.2.a.
508.text: This includes the time preceding and following the game, when members from teams are present in the vicinity of the field.

510.label: C1.B.1.
510.text: A Technical Foul can be assessed against a team for minor conduct violations that do not affect competitiveness or Spirit of the Game.

511.label: C1.B.2.
511.text: Technical Fouls shall be assessed at the next stoppage of play (3.M), or when the current scoring attempt (3.K) is completed.

512.label: C1.B.3.
512.text: There is no limit to the number of Technical Fouls a team can accrue during a game.

513.label: C1.B.4.
513.text: Technical Fouls do not carry over beyond the game in which they are issued.

515.label: C1.C.1.
515.text: A blue card can be assessed against a team for conduct that affects competitiveness or violates Spirit of the Game by any team member or spectators considered as partisans for the team.

516.label: C1.C.2.
516.text: The blue card can be assessed regardless of whether the infracted team makes any call, although teams are encouraged to make violation calls in order to directly communicate about issues and minimize the need for observers to issue blue cards without a call.

517.label: C1.C.3.
517.text: Blue cards shall be assessed at the next stoppage of play (3.M), or when the current scoring attempt (3.K) is completed.

518.label: C1.C.4.
518.text: There is no limit to the number of blue cards a team can accrue during a game.

519.label: C1.C.5.
519.text: Blue cards do not carry over beyond the game in which they are issued.

521.label: C1.D.1.
521.text: A yellow card can be assessed against a specific team member for particularly egregious conduct or a pattern of such behavior. A yellow card is a formal warning for unacceptable behavior and puts the team member on notice that any further such actions will result in ejection from the game.

522.label: C1.D.2.
522.text: The yellow card can be assessed regardless of whether the infracted team makes any call, although teams are encouraged to make violation calls in order to directly communicate about issues and minimize the need for observers to issue yellow cards without a call.

523.label: C1.D.3.
523.text: Observers may stop play to issue a yellow card.

524.label: C1.D.3.a.
524.text: If play is stopped to assess a yellow card, for the purpose of the Continuation Rule (17.C), the situation should be treated like an injury called at the time of the infraction.

525.label: C1.D.3.b.
525.text: Observers may delay the stoppage until the infracted team is no longer in possession of the disc.

526.label: C1.D.3.c.
526.text: Observers may issue a yellow card during a subsequent stoppage if an observer consultation is needed to determine whether a yellow card is warranted.

527.label: C1.D.4.
527.text: A yellow card is non-reviewable for the duration of the game, although it may be appealed to the EDA after the game.

528.label: C1.D.5.
528.text: One blue card is automatically assessed against a team whenever one of its members receives a yellow card.

530.label: C1.E.1.
530.text: A red card can be assessed against a specific team member for particularly egregious conduct that warrants immediate suspension from the game.

531.label: C1.E.2.
531.text: No formal or informal warning is necessary before a team member is issued a red card, and a red card need not be preceded by a blue card or yellow card.

532.label: C1.E.3.
532.text: Observers may stop play to assess a red card, and should stop as soon as possible.

533.label: C1.E.3.a.
533.text: If observers stop play to issue a red card, for the purpose of the Continuation Rule (17.C), the situation should be treated like an injury at the time of the infraction.

534.label: C1.E.3.b.
534.text: Observers may issue a red card during a subsequent stoppage if an observer consultation is needed to determine whether a red card is warranted.

535.label: C1.E.4.
535.text: A red card is non-reviewable for the duration of the game, although it may be appealed to the EDA after the game.

536.label: C1.E.5.
536.text: Two blue cards are automatically assessed against a team whenever one of its members receives a red card.

538.label: C2.A.
538.text: Teams are encouraged to demonstrate good Spirit (2.E) and resolve disagreements between themselves. When such a resolution is not possible, or when behavior clearly violates Spirit of the Game (2.F), observers may assess Misconduct Fouls.

539.label: C2.B.
539.text: The following is a non-exhaustive list of behaviors that may result in Misconduct Fouls. When warranted by the severity or circumstances of the conduct, more serious penalties than those illustrated may be applied.

541.label: C2.C.1.
541.text: All observer-ruled or uncontested player-called dangerous plays (20.B) shall receive a yellow card.

542.label: C2.C.2.
542.text: Dangerous plays that are particularly egregious (reasonable certainty of violent contact with a player’s knees or head, or other injurious behavior) may result in a red card.

544.label: C2.D.1.
544.text: Intentionally striking another person, or any clear attempt to do so, warrants a red card. This includes, but is not limited to: punching or attempting to punch, kicking or attempting to kick, spitting on or attempting to spit on someone.

546.label: C2.E.1.
546.text: Aggressively pushing or shoving another player, or other acts of physical aggression warrant a yellow card or red card.

548.label: C2.F.1.
548.text: Repeated instances of the same infraction may receive a blue card.

549.label: C2.F.2.
549.text: Continued instances of a repeated infraction after a blue card has been issued (C2.F.1) may receive a yellow card.

551.label: C2.G.1.
551.text: Egregious instances of any infraction may receive a blue card or yellow card.

553.label: C2.H.1.
553.text: Deliberate infractions may receive a yellow card.

555.label: C2.I.1.
555.text: Calls or contests that are knowingly incorrect or made with disregard for their validity may result in a blue card.

556.label: C2.I.2.
556.text: Continued instances of calls or contests that are knowingly incorrect or made with disregard for their validity after a blue card has been issued (C2.I.1) may receive a yellow card.

558.label: C2.J.1.
558.text: Taunting an opponent or spiking the disc towards a player warrants either a blue card or yellow card, depending on the severity of the behavior.

559.label: C2.J.2.
559.text: Forcibly spiking, or attempting to forcibly spike a disc on a player is considered intentionally striking (C2.D.1).

560.label: C2.J.3.
560.text: If a team member spikes the disc without intending to hit another person, and it does hit an opposing team member, a blue card or yellow card may be assessed at the discretion of the observer.

562.label: C2.K.1.
562.text: Verbal abuse directed at a player, game official, spectator, or tournament official warrants a blue card or yellow card, depending on the severity and extent of the language. This includes continued dissent with observer rulings.

563.label: C2.K.2.
563.text: Egregious verbal abuse such as racial and gender based slurs warrants a red card.

565.label: C2.L.1.
565.text: General undirected swearing in accordance with specific written guidelines for a tournament warrants a Technical Foul.

567.label: C2.M.1.
567.text: Sideline encroachment that does not affect an observer’s ability to make a call warrants a Technical Foul.

568.label: C2.M.2.
568.text: Sideline encroachment that interferes with play or affects an observer’s ability to make a call warrants a blue card.

569.label: C2.M.3.
569.text: Non-player (3.Q.10) team members entering the playing field before a goal has been signaled by an observer warrants a Technical Foul.

570.label: C2.N.
570.text: Any other behavior described by the USAU Code of Conduct may warrant a misconduct foul at the discretion of the observer.

571.label: C2.O.
571.text: Any behavior that would warrant the issuance of a misconduct foul, but which occurs in a game without observers, can result in sanctions upon a complaint filed to the EDA.

573.label: C3.A.
573.text: This section does not limit additional sanctions that may be issued by the EDA after a game.

575.label: C3.B.1.
575.text: A team member shall receive a game suspension upon receiving

576.label: C3.B.1.a.
576.text: two yellow cards during a game, or

578.label: C3.B.2.
578.text: If a team member receives a game suspension in the second half of a game, the suspension remains in effect for the first half of the team’s next game.

579.label: C3.B.3.
579.text: A team member shall be suspended for the remainder of a tournament upon receiving:

580.label: C3.B.3.a.
580.text: three yellow cards in a tournament, or

581.label: C3.B.3.b.
581.text: one yellow card and one red card in a tournament, or

582.label: C3.B.3.c.
582.text: two red cards in a tournament.

583.label: C3.B.4.
583.text: A suspended team member must immediately leave the general area where their game is being played, as directed by the EDA.

584.label: C3.B.4.a.
584.text: In practice this means the team member must remove themselves at least 100 yards from their team’s current playing field and refrain from interacting with any team members, spectators, or officials involved in that game.

585.label: C3.B.4.b.
585.text: Failure to do so results in a forfeit for that team member’s team.

586.label: C3.B.5.
586.text: If a team member participates in a game from which they have been suspended, that team member is suspended for the entire tournament, and the team member’s team forfeits that game.

587.label: C3.B.6.
587.text: A team whose player (3.Q.10) is suspended may substitute another player, and the opposing team also may exchange a player if they wish.

588.label: C3.B.7.
588.text: All players must remain in the positions they occupied when play stopped, unless the ejection also triggers a Team Misconduct Penalty (C3.C).

589.label: C3.B.8.
589.text: A suspension is non-reviewable for the duration of the game when it was assessed, although it may be appealed to the EDA after the game.

590.label: C3.B.9.
590.text: A suspension does not carry over to subsequent tournaments.

592.label: C3.C.1.
592.text: A Team Misconduct Penalty shall be assessed against a team when a Technical Foul is assessed against a team, and the team previously accumulated two or more Technical Fouls.

593.label: C3.C.2.
593.text: A Team Misconduct Penalty shall be assessed against a team when a blue card is assessed against a team, and the team has previously accumulated two or more blue cards.

594.label: C3.C.3.
594.text: A Team Misconduct Penalty shall be assessed at the next stoppage of play (3.M), or when the current scoring attempt (3.K) is completed. This includes stoppages initiated due to a yellow card (C1.D.3) or red card (C1.E.3).

595.label: C3.C.4.
595.text: When a Team Misconduct Penalty is assessed against the offense, the defense has two options available to continue play.

596.label: C3.C.4.a.
596.text: The disc is moved to the reverse brick mark, away from the end zone of attack. The offense is given 30 seconds to set up anywhere on the playing field. After all offensive players have assumed stationary positions, the defense has an additional 20 seconds to match up and check the disc in with a stall count of one.

597.label: C3.C.4.b.
597.text: The disc and players stay where they were when play stopped. Once players are ready, the count resumes at the stall count reached plus one, or at nine if over eight.

598.label: C3.C.5.
598.text: When a Team Misconduct Penalty is assessed against the defense, the offense has three options available to continue play.

599.label: C3.C.5.a.
599.text: The disc is moved to the brick mark closest to the end zone of attack. The offense has 30 seconds to set up anywhere on the playing field. After all offensive players have assumed stationary positions, the defense has an additional 20 seconds to match up and check the disc in with a stall count of one.

600.label: C3.C.5.b.
600.text: The disc is centered on the long axis of the field. The offense has 30 seconds to set up anywhere on the playing field. After all offensive players have assumed stationary positions, the defense has an additional 20 seconds to match up and check the disc in with a stall count of one.

601.label: C3.C.5.c.
601.text: The disc and players stay where they were when play stopped. Once players are ready, the defense checks in the disc with a stall count of one.

602.label: C3.C.6.
602.text: Team Misconduct Penalty Assessed Between Points

603.label: C3.C.6.a.
603.text: If a Team Misconduct Penalty is assessed after the completion of a point, there is no pull.

604.label: C3.C.6.b.
604.text: If the penalty is against the receiving team, the disc is put into play at the brick mark in the end zone they are defending (C3.C.4.a).

605.label: C3.C.6.c.
605.text: If the penalty is against the pulling team, the receiving team puts the disc into play at the brick mark closest to the end zone they are attacking (C3.C.5.a).

606.label: C3.C.6.d.
606.text: Each team may substitute players as per 8.A.1.

607.label: C3.C.6.e.
607.text: Each offensive player must establish a stationary position by 90 seconds after the previous goal was scored.

608.label: C3.C.6.f.
608.text: The defense has 100 seconds after the previous goal was scored or up to 20 seconds after all offensive players have established their positions (whichever is longer) to check the disc into play.

610.label: C3.C.7.a.
610.text: A captain (3.Q.1) can decline a misconduct penalty and leave the disc as is.

611.label: C3.C.7.b.
611.text: In exceptional circumstances, observers may deny the declination if they feel the teams are trying to circumvent mandatory tournament rules, guidelines, or player safety.

613.label: C3.D.1.
613.text: If five yellow cards are assessed against team members on a single team during a game, that team forfeits the game. For this purpose, a red card is equivalent to two yellow cards. For example, if three or more players on a single team are assessed red cards, that team forfeits the game.

614.label: C3.D.2.
614.text: If the situation arises where both teams would be required to forfeit the game (C3.D.1) due to multiple simultaneously assessed misconduct fouls, the EDA determines the appropriate outcome based on competition considerations.

615.label: D1.
615.text: Player and Observer Hand Signals In- or out-of-bounds Disc up Disc down In the end zone Goal Force-out foul Bobble Foul No contest Contest Retraction (3 swipes) Injury timeout Injury substitution Disc uncatchable Pick Travel Double team Disc space Fast count Vision blocking Stall or time violation Readiness or brick Play has stopped Announce stall (use appropriate number of fingers) Gender ratio: 4 women-matching players Gender ratio: 4 men-matching players Offsides (Arms straight, upward at a 45 degree angle, hands open, forming a “V” with your arms) False start or violation 10 seconds 20 seconds Pre-stall count Timeout Spirit timeout

616.label: D2.
616.text: Observer Only Hand Signals Observer ruling Observer overrule Call on field Timeout

618.label: E1.A.
618.text: There are not currently any requirements for rules adaptations for youth under this edition of the rules. Requirements may come in future editions based on continued research and experience with adapting the sport for youth age groups.

620.label: E2.A.
620.text: These rule adaptations are not required, but are optional recommendations taken in whole or in part, to help younger players learn to play in a more developmentally appropriate environment.

622.label: F1.A.
622.text: The recommended number of players per team is either five (5) or four (4).

625.label: F2.A.1.
625.text: The standard field of play, including end zones, is 82 yards (75 meters) long and 27 yards (25 meters) wide.

626.label: F2.A.2.
626.text: The standard end zone is 16 yards (15 meters) deep.

627.label: F2.A.3.
627.text: The brick marks are located 11 yards (10 meters) from the goal line.

629.label: F2.B.1.
629.text: The standard field of play, including end zones, is 50 yards (45 meters) long and 30 yards (27 meters) wide.

630.label: F2.B.2.
630.text: The standard end zone is 8 yards (7.5 meters) deep.

631.label: F2.B.3.
631.text: The brick marks are located 8 yards (7.5 meters) from the goal line.

632.label: F2.C.
632.text: Perimeter and end zone lines are established with field lining tape (preferred) or rope.

633.label: F2.C.1.
633.text: The top of the tape is the portion of the field tape that is facing upwards. Should the tape twist along its length, the “top” is always the side that is facing up, even if that changes along the length.

634.label: F2.D.
634.text: The top of the perimeter lines is not part of the playing field and is out-of-bounds.

635.label: F2.E.
635.text: The area directly under the perimeter lines and all parts of the perimeter lines excluding the top of the tape are part of the playing field and in-bounds.

636.label: F2.F.
636.text: The top of the goal line is part of the central zone.

637.label: F2.G.
637.text: The area directly under the goal line and all parts of the goal line excluding the top of the tape are part of the end zone.

639.label: F3.A.
639.text: Shoes and cleats are not allowed. Players’ feet must be bare or only have a soft covering such as socks or tape.

648.label: F5.A.
648.text: Any player may briefly extend a stoppage of play to remove interfering sand from their face, but active play may not be stopped for this purpose.

650.label: F6.A.
650.text: A sand foul occurs when a player causes sand to fly into an opponent’s face in a way that significantly interferes with their play, such as in the eyes or up their nose.

651.label: F6.B.
651.text: A sand foul is considered distinct and separate to the action that caused it and should be resolved separately as such.
